name: Permissive CORS — reflected Origin with credentials allowed
description: Sends a cross-site request carrying an attacker-controlled Origin and checks the response. When the server reflects that arbitrary Origin back into Access-Control-Allow-Origin AND sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, any website the victim visits can make credentialed cross-site requests to this API and read the responses — the classic CORS account-takeover / data-exfiltration misconfiguration (a reflected origin defeats the same-origin policy that credentials otherwise rely on). The predicate requires BOTH the reflected origin and the credentials flag, so a correctly-scoped CORS policy (fixed allow-list, or credentials off) does not trip it.

vulnerability.remediation: Never reflect the request Origin into Access-Control-Allow-Origin while Allow-Credentials is true. Use a fixed server-side allow-list of trusted origins and echo the Origin ONLY when it matches; otherwise omit the header. ASP.NET Core: `app.UseCors(p => p.WithOrigins("https://app.example.com").AllowCredentials())` — never `.AllowAnyOrigin()` combined with `.AllowCredentials()` (the framework blocks that pairing, but hand-rolled reflection middleware bypasses the guard). If the API is public and credential-free, drop Allow-Credentials entirely.
